request.question: A mythical forest creature that is seductive and from Sweden has the same being as what divine figure?

title: Shaykh Haydar
Chullachaki
Nariphon
Skogsrå
Silili
Cura (mythology)
Thalna
Almaz (mythical beast)
Oceanus
Hulder
sentences: Shaykh Haydar or Sheikh Haydar (Persian: شیخ حیدر‎ ‎ "Shaikh Ḥaidar"; b. 1459, Amed - d. 9 July 1488, Tabasaran) was the successor of his father (Shaykh Junayd) as leader of the Safaviyya from 1460-1488.
Haydar maintained the policies and political ambitions initiated by his father.

Under Sheikh Haydar, the Safaviyya became crystallized as a political movement with an increasingly extremist heterodox Twelver Shi'i coloring and Haydar was viewed as a divine figure by his followers.

Shaykh Haydar was responsible for instructing his followers to adopt the scarlet headgear of 12 gores commemorating The Twelve Imams, which led to them being designated by the Turkish term Qizilbash "Red Head".

The Chullachaki (Quechua, "one-footed", from "chulla" or "ch'ulla" = single, odd, unpaired, asymmetric, "chaki" = foot; spelling sometimes also used in Spanish) or Chullachaqui (hispanicized spelling), also known as the Shapishico, is a mythical forest creature of the Peruvian and Brazilian Amazonian jungle.
The Nariphon (Thai: นารีผล ), also known as Makkaliphon (Thai: มักกะลีผล , from Pali "makkaliphala"), is a tree in Buddhist mythology which bears fruit in the shape of young female creatures.
The maidens grow attached by their head from the tree branches.
This tree grows at the Himaphan, a mythical forest where the female fruits are enjoyed by the Gandharvas who cut the fruits and take them away.

The skogsrå , (Forest Rå), "Skogsfrun" (The Mistress of the Forest), "Skogssnuvan", "Skogsnymfen" (Forest Nymph), "Råndan" (the Rå) or "huldra", was a mythical creature of the forest, or Rå, in Swedish folklore.
Silili is a divine figure mentioned in Tablet VI of the Epic of Gilgamesh.
Gilgamesh objects to the unwanted attractions of the goddess Ishtar, enumerating the previous lovers of Ishtar, and the misfortunes which befell them.
He claims that Ishtar once loved the horse, but ultimately decreed a number of misfortunes for him, including the continuous wailing of "his mother, Silili".
Because Silili is only mentioned in passing, there is little to say of her character, other than she is a female divine figure closely associated with the horse.
Cura is the name of a divine figure whose name means "Care" or "Concern" in Latin.
Hyginus seems to have created both the personification and story for his "Fabulae", poem 220.
In Etruscan religion and myth, Thalna was a divine figure usually regarded as a goddess of childbirth.

Determinate gender, however, is not necessarily a characteristic of Etruscan deities, and Thalna is also either depicted as male, or seems to be identified as a male figure because of the placement of names around a scene.
Her other functions include friendship and prophecy.
Her name may mean "growth, bloom."
She appears in Etruscan art in the company of Turan, Tinia, and Menrva.

The almaz (Chechen: "almaz" or "hun-sag"; Adyghe: "almesti"), roughly translated as various "feral forest-man" or "stone spirit", is a mythical beast that is considered to be an evil forest creature with magical powers residing in its hair that exists in Chechen and Ingush folk beliefs and Circassian folk beliefs.
The first "attestation" of it in writing was by a Bavarian captive of the Mongols, but it is present in the national folklore of Chechens and Ingush and of Circassians.

The male almaz is said to be hairy and hideous, and have an axe embedded in its chest, while the female is very beautiful with large breasts and golden hair, and has a "favorite pastime" of dancing naked at night under the moon.
The almaz is said to have magical powers residing in its hair, but if the hair is removed or even grabbed, it may be rendered helpless.

It has been theoried by some to have arisen under Mongolian influence, either during the Mongol invasions of Dzurdzuketia or the intervening period where the northern Dzurdzuk state of Simsir was subjugated to the Mongol-controlled Golden Horde.
The word "almaz" is a loan from Mongolian where it originally meant "forest man".
Amjad Jaimoukha however suggested that the name "almaz" may have started to have been used by North Caucasians for an already existent native concept during the sojourn of the Golden Horde of Simsir.

Oceanus ( ; Greek: Ὠκεανός "Ōkeanós", ] ), also known as Ogenus ("Ogenos", Ωγηνος) or Ogen (Ωγην), was a divine figure in classical antiquity, believed by the ancient Greeks and Romans to be the divine personification of the sea, an enormous river encircling the world.
A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore.
(Her name derives from a root meaning "covered" or "secret".)
In Norwegian folklore, she is known as huldra ("the [archetypal] hulder", though folklore presupposes that there is an entire Hulder race and not just a single individual).
She is known as the skogsrå "forest spirit" or Tallemaja "pine tree Mary" in Swedish folklore, and ulda in Sámi folklore.
Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: A mythical forest creature that is seductive and from Sweden has the same being as what divine figure?
hop1_docs: Hulder | A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore.  (Her name derives from a root meaning "covered" or "secret".)  In Norwegian folklore, she is known as huldra ("the [archetypal] hulder", though folklore presupposes that there is an entire Hulder race and not just a single individual).  She is known as the skogsrå "forest spirit" or Tallemaja "pine tree Mary" in Swedish folklore, and ulda in Sámi folklore.  Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda.

Gryttie | Gryttie is a mythical creature which is reputed to dwell in the depths of Gryttjen, in Hälsingland, Sweden.  Gryttjen Lake is located fairly deep in the forest between the towns of Ljusdal and Hudiksvall.

List of Greek mythological figures | The following is a list of gods, goddesses and many other divine and semi-divine figures from Ancient Greek mythology and Ancient Greek religion.  (The list does not include creatures; for these, see List of Greek mythological creatures.)

Peder Jönsson | Peder Jönsson (died 1640), was a Swedish hunter and fisherman from Söderköping, who was executed for having confessed to sexual intercourse with a Sjörået (a lake-nymph; a mythical female creature of the lake).  Though they are other cases of the same kind in 17th-century Sweden, his was the only case were the sentence is confirmed to have been executed.

Iele | The Iele are feminine mythical creatures In Romanian mythology.  There are several differing descriptions of their characteristics.  Often they are described as virgin fairies ("zâne" in Romanian), with great seductive power over men, with magic skills and attributes similar to Nymphs, Naiads and Dryads found in Greek mythology.  They are also similar to the Samodivas in Bulgaria

Coco (folklore) | The Coco (or Cuco, Coca, Cuca, Cucuy) is a mythical ghost-monster, equivalent to the bogeyman, found in many Hispanic and Lusophone countries.  He can also be considered a Hispanic version of a bugbear, as it is a commonly used figure of speech representing an irrational or exaggerated fear.  The Coco is a male being while Coca is the female version of the mythical monster, although it is not possible to distinguish one from the other as both are the representation of the same being.

Pombero | The Pombéro is a mythical humanoid creature of small stature being from Guaraní mythology.  The legend, along with other mythological figures from the Guaraní, is an important part of the culture in the region spanning from northeast Argentina northward through the whole of Paraguay and southern Brazil.  The Pombéro is said to capture particularly ungrateful girls, and force them to kiss him, and later, force them to have sexual intercourse with him.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Hulder (Scandinavian seductive forest creature)

- Fact: "A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore."
- Fact: Swedish variants: she "is known as the skogsrå 'forest spirit' or Tallemaja 'pine tree Mary' in Swedish folklore."
- Fact: "Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda."
- Ambiguity/missing: multiple name variants appear (hulder, huldra, skogsrå, Tallemaja, ulda) — these are candidate forms for the same entity; no other divine-figure candidates are presented.
- Next-hop retrieval (priority order):
  1. Huld (völva) — fetch page lede/infobox and "Origins/Name" or "Etymology" sections to confirm identity.
  2. Holda / Frau Holda — fetch lede and "Origins" / "Folklore" sections for German links.
  3. Hulder — inspect lede, "Name" / "Etymology" and "Swedish variants" (sections mentioning skogsrå/Tallemaja).
  4. Skogsrå and Tallemaja — fetch their pages/sections (Swedish folklore entries) to confirm naming and equivalence.

No additional retrieval strictly needed to see the claim in the provided passages, but retrieve the above pages to verify and gather supporting details for a final answer.
hop2_docs: Hulder | A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore.  (Her name derives from a root meaning "covered" or "secret".)  In Norwegian folklore, she is known as huldra ("the [archetypal] hulder", though folklore presupposes that there is an entire Hulder race and not just a single individual).  She is known as the skogsrå "forest spirit" or Tallemaja "pine tree Mary" in Swedish folklore, and ulda in Sámi folklore.  Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda.

Huld | In Scandinavian mythology, Huld is only referenced by "völva" or "seiðkona", that is a woman who practiced the seiðr.  She is mentioned in the "Ynglinga saga", "Sturlunga saga" and a late medieval Icelandic tale.  In the latter source, she is Odin's mistress and the mother of the demi-goddesses Þorgerðr and Irpa.  As her name suggests, Huld may be in origin the same being as the Huldra and the German Holda.

Coat of arms of Lardal | The coat of arms of Lardal was approved on 17 July 1992 and is a gold hulder on a red field.  The flag was at the same time approved as a yellow hulder on a red field.  Both the coat of arms and flag were designed by the artist Arvid Sveen.

Hafdís Huld | Hafdís Huld Þrastardóttir (born 22 May 1979), known simply as Hafdís Huld, is an Icelandic singer and actress.  She began her musical career as a member of the electronic band GusGus in 1995 and left the group in 1999.  Hafdís made her solo debut with her 2006 album "Dirty Paper Cup".

Origin of language | The origin of language in the human species has been the topic of scholarly discussions for several centuries.  There is no consensus on the origin or age of human language.  The topic is difficult to study because of the lack of direct evidence.  Consequently, scholars wishing to study the origins of language must draw inferences from other kinds of evidence such as the fossil record, archaeological evidence, contemporary language diversity, studies of language acquisition, and comparisons between human language and systems of communication existing among other animals (particularly other primates).  Many argue that the origins of language probably relate closely to the origins of modern human behavior, but there is little agreement about the implications and directionality of this connection.

Palle Huld | Palle Huld (2 August 1912 – 26 November 2010) was a Danish film actor and writer.  He appeared in 40 films between 1933 and 2000.  He was born in Hellerup in Denmark.  His journey around the world at the age of 15 in 1928 reportedly inspired Hergé to create Tintin.

Vegvísir | A Vegvísir (Icelandic 'sign post' or 'wayfinder') is an Icelandic magical stave intended to help the bearer find their way through rough weather.  The symbol is attested in the Huld Manuscript, collected in Iceland by Geir Vigfusson in 1880 (but consisting of material of earlier origin).
response.summary_2: Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda. Huld is attested in Scandinavian sources as a völva (a seiðkona, a practitioner of seiðr). Holda is a German folkloric figure linked to similar traditions.
response.answer: The völva divine figure Huld (also identified with the German Holda).
